title: Psychiatric Comorbidity in DSM-III-R Hypochondriasis
abstract:
Psychiatric Comorbidity in DSM-III-R Hypochondriasis
Arthur J. Barsky, MD; Grace Wyshak, PhD; Gerald L. Klerman, MD
\s=b\Forty-two DSM-III-R hypochondriacs from
a general
medical clinic were compared with a random sample of 76
outpatients from the same setting. Patients completed a re-
search battery that included a structured diagnostic inter-
view (Diagnostic Interview Schedule) and self-report ques-
tionnaires
to
measure personality
disorder
caseness,
functional impairment, and hypochondriacal symptoms.
Psychiatric morbidity in the hypochondriacal sample sig-
nificantly exceeded that of the comparison sample. Hypo-
chondriacs had twice as many lifetime Axis I diagnoses,
twice as many Diagnostic Interview Schedule symptoms,
and three times the level of personality disorder caseness as
the comparison group. Of the hypochondriacal sample,
88% had one or more additional Axis I disorders, the over-
lap being greatest with depressive and anxiety disorders.
One fifth of the hypochondriacs had somatization disorder,
but the two conditions appeared to be phenomenologically
distinct. Hypochondriacal patients with coexisting anxiety
and/or depressive disorder (secondary hypochondriasis) did
not differ greatly from hypochondriacal patients without
these comorbid conditions (primary hypochondriasis). Be-
cause the nature of hypochondriasis remains unclear and
requires further study, we suggest that its nosologic status
not be altered in DSM-IV.
(Arch Gen Psychiatry. 1992;49:101-108)
The
DSM-III-R description of hypochondriasis is widely
accepted. However, it is based on surprisingly little
empiric data, since the phenomenology, epidemiology,
and nosology of hypochondriasis have not yet been
definitively established.
DSM-III-R hypochondriasis is characterized by the fear or
belief that one has a serious disease; physical signs or
symptoms for which there is no medical explanation; the
ineffectiveness ofappropriate reassurance; and a duration of
greater than 6 months. The symptoms must not occur only
during panic attacks and must not be delusional. This
description, however, does not encompass other hypo-
chondriacal syndromes encountered in medical and psychi¬
atric settings. The Figure outlines this broader spectrum of
hypochondriacal conditions. In primary hypochondriasis,
no other psychiatric disorder is present, or, if present, the
comorbid condition is unrelated to and independent of hy¬
pochondriasis. DSM-III-R hypochondriasis is one of two
types of primary hypochondriasis. The other is monosymp-
tomatic hypochondriasis, which is characterized by a single,
fixed, delusional belief that one is diseased. Secondary hy¬
pochondriasis occurs as an ancillary feature of a more per¬
vasive or dominant condition or is a response to a stressful
precipitant, such as a life-threatening medical illness or the
death of someone important. Primary and secondary arebeing
used here to refer to the concurrent presence or absence of
another disorder with hypochondriasis. In this article, the
terms do not refer to any presumed cause-and-effect rela¬
tionship between two disorders or to the chronologic se¬
quence in which they developed.
HYPOCHONDRIASIS AND OTHER CONCURRENT
PSYCHIATRIC DISORDER
There are three major nosologie controversies about
DSM-III-R hypochondriasis: the degree to which primary
hypochondriasis exists, ie, the degree to which it occurs
in the absence of other psychopathologic features; the ex¬
tent of overlap between hypochondriasis and anxiety
disorders, depression, and somatization disorder; and the
relationship between hypochondriasis and Axis II disor¬
der.
Although DSM-III-R accords hypochondriasis indepen¬
dent status as a primary disorder, the empiric data on this
are inconsistent. It is not clear how common primary hy¬
pochondriasis is relative to secondary hypochondriasis,
or even whether it exists at all.1"7 The proportion of hypo¬
chondriacal patients who have no other major concurrent
psychiatric disorder has been reported to range from very
small2 to 36%,7 45%,6 and 58%.] There are at least two
sources of this persistent confusion. First, longitudinal
studies are lacking, and without them it is difficult to de¬
termine which of the disorders came first. Second, differ¬
ent populations have been studied. Primary hypochon¬
driacs
are
more likely to be encountered in studies
conducted in medical settings, believing as they do that
their problem is exclusively medical. Secondary hypo¬
chondriacs, on the other hand, are more often encoun¬
tered in studies of psychiatric populations, where they
have come seeking treatment for their comorbid psychi¬
atric conditions.
Accepted for publication December 19, 1990.
From the Departments of Psychiatry (Dr Barsky) and Medicine (Dr
Wyshak), Harvard Medical School, Boston, Mass; Psychiatry Service
and Primary Care Program, Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston
(Dr Barsky); Department of Biostatistics and Center for Population
Studies, Harvard School of Public Health (Dr Wyshak); and Depart-
ment of Psychiatry, Cornell University Medical College, New York,
NY (Dr Klerman).
Reprint requests to Massachusetts General Hospital, Warren 631,
Fruit Street, Boston, MA 02114 (Dr Barsky).

Hypochondriasis
The hypochondriacal syndrome.
Clinical surveys point to considerable overlap between
hypochondriasis
and
anxiety,1'8"14
depression,1,15"22
obsessive-compulsive disorder,
and phobic disorders.24
Hypochondriacs by DSM-III criteria have more anxiety
symptoms than do matched nonhypochondriacal psychi¬
atric outpatients.13 Patients with panic disorder have el¬
evated somatization scores, elevated pain complaints, and
hypochondriacal fears that decline with treatment of the
panic.8 Indeed, patients with panic disorder scored almost
as high on a hypochondriasis questionnaire as hypochon¬
driacal psychiatric inpatients did,10 and their hypochon¬
driacal symptoms responded to treatment of the anxiety
disorder. Recently, cognitive researchers proposed a the¬
oretical similarity in the two conditions: the misinterpre¬
tation or "catastrophizing" of somatic symptoms.10,26
The situation with respect to depression is similar. In a
retrospective chart review of hypochondriacal inpatients,
82% of those with secondary hypochondriasis were di¬
agnosed as depressed.1 Two thirds of hypochondriacal
outpatients score in the clinically depressed range on the
Zung Self-rating Depression Scale.
Depressed medical
and surgical inpatients are more hypochondriacal than
nondepressed patients, and their depression scores are
highly correlated with disease conviction and general hy¬
pochondriasis.17 Among general medical outpatients, de¬
pressive symptoms predicted 33% of the variance in hy¬
pochondriasis, and the two had a zero-order correlation
of0.58 (P =. 0001).18 These associations among anxiety, de¬
pressive, and hypochondriacal symptoms have also been
confirmed in other samples of medical patients and in stu¬
dents and employees who were not unusually hypochon¬
driacal.13,17
Another controversy involves the overlap between hy¬
pochondriasis and other somatoform disorders. The DSM-
III represented the first attempt to group all the somato¬
form conditions together in a single class of disorder, to
differentiate them from other psychiatric disorders, and to
define them relative to each other. But this taxonomy lacks
empiric validation. We do not know how distinct and dis¬
crete these categories actually are in clinical reality. The
distinction between hypochondriasis and somatization
disorder, for example, is unclear. The descriptions of these
two entities have arisen from different conceptual tradi¬
tions (the psychodynamic and the descriptive), and it is
possible that hypochondriasis and somatization disorder
are two different ways of describing the same clinical en¬
tity—like the proverbial blind men describing different as¬
pects of the same elephant. Many clinicians have the im¬
pression that these two disorders can be differentiated,
and some data suggest that although the overlap between
them is considerable, it is by no means complete. Hypo¬
chondriacal symptoms, for example, were found in 38%
offamily practice patients with somatization disorder.27 In
a study ofhypochondriacal patients referred to psychiatry
from general practitioners, 39% satisfied the criteria for
somatization disorder.28 Epidemiologie work suggests
that hypochondriacal patients and patients with somati¬
zation disorder can be distinguished on the basis of symp¬
toms, disability, sociodemographic characteristics, and as¬
sociated psychopathologic features.29
The relationship between hypochondriasis and person¬
ality is even less clear. Hypochondriasis itself has been
thought of as having some of the characteristics of a per¬
sonality style or trait,30"32 since it is an enduring way of
dealing with the external world, relating to other people,
and responding to life stress. From this perspective, it is
viewed as a constellation of health-related attitudes, a per¬
ceptual style, and a set of beliefs, which are experienced
as ego syntonic and an integral part of the individual's
identity. This view is summed up in the clinical aphorism:
"the hypochondriac does not just have his symptoms; he
is his symptoms." Alternatively, Axis I hypochondriacs
may be associated with other particular Axis II disor¬
ders.10,30,33 The hypochondriac's investment of interest,
attention, and psychic energy in his or her own body, to
the exclusion of other people and objects, seems pro¬
foundly narcissistic.34 Self-psychologists have conceptu¬
alized the hypochondriac's bodily preoccupation as an at¬
tempt
to

and
integrate

disorder. Recently, cognitive researchers proposed a the¬
oretical similarity in the two conditions: the misinterpre¬
tation or "catastrophizing" of somatic symptoms.10,26

Another controversy involves the overlap between hy¬
pochondriasis and other somatoform disorders. The DSM-
III represented the first attempt to group all the somato¬
form conditions together in a single class of disorder, to
differentiate them from other psychiatric disorders, and to
define them relative to each other. But this taxonomy lacks
empiric validation. We do not know how distinct and dis¬
crete these categories actually are in clinical reality. The
distinction between hypochondriasis and somatization
disorder, for example, is unclear. The descriptions of these
two entities have arisen from different conceptual tradi¬
tions (the psychodynamic and the descriptive), and it is
possible that hypochondriasis and somatization disorder
are two different ways of describing the same clinical en¬
tity—like the proverbial blind men describing different as¬
pects of the same elephant. Many clinicians have the im¬
pression that these two disorders can be differentiated,
and some data suggest that although the overlap between
them is considerable, it is by no means complete. Hypo¬
chondriacal symptoms, for example, were found in 38%
offamily practice patients with somatization disorder.27 In
a study ofhypochondriacal patients referred to psychiatry
from general practitioners, 39% satisfied the criteria for
somatization disorder.28 Epidemiologie work suggests
that hypochondriacal patients and patients with somati¬
zation disorder can be distinguished on the basis of symp¬
toms, disability, sociodemographic characteristics, and as¬
sociated psychopathologic features.29
The relationship between hypochondriasis and person¬
ality is even less clear. Hypochondriasis itself has been
thought of as having some of the characteristics of a per¬
sonality style or trait,30"32 since it is an enduring way of
dealing with the external world, relating to other people,
and responding to life stress. From this perspective, it is
viewed as a constellation of health-related attitudes, a per¬
ceptual style, and a set of beliefs, which are experienced
as ego syntonic and an integral part of the individual's
identity. This view is summed up in the clinical aphorism:
"the hypochondriac does not just have his symptoms; he
is his symptoms." Alternatively, Axis I hypochondriacs
may be associated with other particular Axis II disor¬
ders.10,30,33 The hypochondriac's investment of interest,
attention, and psychic energy in his or her own body, to
the exclusion of other people and objects, seems pro¬
foundly narcissistic.34 Self-psychologists have conceptu¬
alized the hypochondriac's bodily preoccupation as an at¬
disintegrating sense of self.35,36 Other observers have em¬
phasized the anxious, obsessional, and schizoid person¬
ality characteristics of hypochondriacs.1,6,31,33
The aim of this study was to determine the psychiatric
comorbidity found in a representative sample of DSM-
III-R hypochondriacs from an ambulatory medical setting.
Particular attention was paid to the relative frequency of
primary and secondary hypochondriasis, to the preva¬
lence of Axis I disorders in the hypochondriacal sample,
and to the level of Axis II caseness in this group.
The study was conducted in the general medicine clinic of the
Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston. This clinic serves 29 000
patients, who make over 50 000 visits annually. It is staffed by
37 attending physicians and 65 house officers, who provide pri¬
mary rather than specialty medical care. Only the patients of at¬
tending physicians were studied. Patients were eligible if they
had been followed at the hospital for at least 24 months and had
had at least one previous visit to the same internist. The exclusion
criteria were illiteracy, inability to speak English, and major sen¬
sory, cognitive, or communication deficits.
A total of 1938 patients were approached to complete the
screening questionnaire. Of these, 298 (15.4%) did not meet in¬
clusion criteria, 422 (21.8%) declined to participate, and 182
(9.4%) did not complete the questionnaires. Thus, screening data
were obtained on 1036 patients (63.2% of those who met inclusion
criteria). Of these patients, 92 (8.9%) exceeded a predetermined
cutoff score on the screening instrument. Thirty-three (35.9%) of
these patients could not be contacted or declined to return to the
hospital to undergo the research battery. Thus, 59 patients
(64.1%) completed the battery, of whom 41 were diagnosed as
having DSM-III-R hypochondriasis with a structured diagnostic
interview. They constitute the hypochondriacal sample. One
hundred forty patients were randomly selected from those below
the cutoff on screening. Of these, 65 (46.4%) could not be sched¬
uled or declined to undergo the research battery, and 75 (53.6%)
completed it. They constitute the comparison sample. (The
screening questionnaire, the distribution and range of scores,
and its specificity and sensitivity are available on request.)
Consecutive clinic attendere on randomly selected days com¬
pleted the self-report screening questionnaire, composed of the
Whiteley Index and the Somatic Symptom Inventory. Patients
returned at a later date to undergo a battery consisting of self-
structured and semistructured inter¬
views, and perceptual and cognitive tests. A structured diagnos¬
tic interview for riypochondriasis was administered at this time
and served as the criterion standard for diagnosing DSM-III-R
hypochondriasis. All instruments were administered by a mas¬
ter's level research assistant who was blind to the patient's
screening score. The patients' medical records were then audited,
and their primary physicians, who were blind to the research
data, completed a questionnaire about them. Subjects received
Variables and Their Measurement
Hypochondriacal symptoms were assessed with the Whiteley
Index and the Somatic Symptom Inventory. The Whiteley In¬
dex37 consists of 14 hypochondriacal attitudes and concerns. Re¬
sponses are scored on an ordinal scale from 1 to 5. On principal
components analysis it yields three factors: disease fear, disease
conviction, and bodily preoccupation. Its test-retest reliability
established.37"41 The infrascale consistency of the Whiteley Index
85 (Cronbach's a), and the test-retest reliability
was .84 (Pearson's product-moment correlation) over a mean in-
terval of 25.6 days. Hypochondriacal somatic complaints were
assessed with a 26-item Somatic Symptom Inventory composed
of items drawn from the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality In¬
ventory hypochondriasis subscale and the Hopkins Symptom
Checklist somatization subscale.42"44 Responses are scored on an
ordinal scale from 1 to 5. This scale had a test-retest reliability of
.86 (Pearson's product-moment correlation) over a mean interval
of 25.6 days and an intrascale consistency of .95 (Cronbach's a).
In previous work in this setting, the Whiteley Index and Somatic
Symptom Inventory were intercorrelated (r= .52; P= .0001) and
together identified medical outpatients with a syndrome consis¬
tent with DSM-III hypochondriasis.18
Although these questionnaires assess hypochondriacal symp¬
toms, they do not provide a clinical diagnosis ofhypochondriasis.
That was done with a structured diagnostic interview based on
operationalized DSM-III-R criteria. This interview follows the for¬
mat of the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-III-R.45 There
are four probe questions followedby eight questions covering the
four DSM-III-R diagnostic criteria. Based on the responses to
these questions, the interviewer judges whether or not the pa¬
tient meets each of the diagnostic criteria. By means of the joint-
assessment method, interrater agreement on the diagnostic cri¬
teria varied between 88% and 96%. The interview was also shown
to have a significant degree of concurrent, convergent, and dis¬
criminant validity. (This work is the subject of a separate report
Psychiatric disorder was assessed with the Diagnostic Inter¬
view Schedule (DIS), version III-A. This is a highly structured
diagnostic interview that has been widely used to obtain DSM-III
diagnoses.46 It generates most of the major Axis I diagnoses, both
current and lifetime, and is scored by computer. Somatization
disorder is the only somatoform disorder covered in the DIS. For
all analyses, the diagnoses of psychosexual dysfunction, post-
traumatic stress syndrome, and tobacco dependence were omit¬
ted. Diagnoses were made without employing the hierarchical
exclusion criteria of DSM-III.
Personality disorder "caseness" was assessed with the five-
item impairment/distress subscale of the Personality Diagnostic
Questionnaire.47,48 These five dichotomous items have a corre¬
lation of .75 with the total score on the complete 163-item Per¬
sonality Diagnostic Questionnaire and can be used to predict
caseness, ie, the likelihood of any DSM-III personality disorder
being present.49 A score on this subscale of 2 or higher has been
used as a cutoff for predicting a high likelihood of personality
disorder caseness.50 Thus, we can only report on the probability
of the patient's having a personality disorder but cannot address
the specific nature of the disorder.
Aggregate medical morbidity was assessed through an audit
of the patient's medical record. Each medical diagnosis was as¬
signed a weight of 1 through 4, depending on its severity, by
means of explicit, preestablished criteria (1 indicates major; 2,
moderate; 3, minor; and 4, nonspecific complaint). We then cal¬
culated a weighted sum of the number of medical diagnoses
present. This method was shown in pilot work to have satis¬
factory interrater reliability. The patient's physician also rated
current morbidity and future prognosis on a five-point ordinal
scale. These two methods of assessing morbidity have a corre¬
Disability was assessed with the Functional Status Question¬
naire, a valid and reliable self-report instrument developed for
use in ambulatory medical populations.51 The Functional Status
Questionnaire is scored by computer, which provides numerical
ratings from 1 to 100 (maximal function).
Somatosensory amplification, the self-reported sensitivity to a
variety ofunpleasant but nonpathologicalbodily sensations, was
measured with an 11-item questionnaire developed in previous
work.52,53 Responses are scored on a five-point ordinal scale. In
this study it was found to have an intrascale reliability of .82
a test-retest reliability of .79 (Pearson's
product-moment correlation) over a mean interval of 74 days.
*DIS indicates Diagnostic Interview Schedule.
Statistical analyses included t tests and analysis of variance
and covariance for continuous and ordinal data and  2 for cate¬
goric data. Risk ratios were obtained by the Mantel-Haenszel
method. Zero-order Pearson's correlations were computed for
determining the association between two variables; multivariate
regression was used to determine the association between an
outcome variable and two or more predictor variables. Analyses
were done with the use of SAS (Statistical Analysis System) for
personal computers. Significance values are P=s.05 (two tailed).
The mean age (57.1 years), sex distribution (76% female),
marital status (51% married; 49% widowed, divorced, or single),
and social class distribution (17% classes I [highest] and II, 32%
class III, and 51% classes IV and V [lowest]) of the hypochondri¬
acal sample did not differ significantly from those of the
comparison sample. There were significantly more blacks (17.1%
vs 5.3%; odds ratio, 3.65; 95% confidence interval, 1.06 to 12.58;
= .04) in the hypochondriacal sample. This difference persisted
after controlling for social position. The sociodemographics of
this hypochondriacal sample are discussed elsewhere.54 The two
groups did not differ significantly in aggregate medical morbid¬
The DSM-III-R hypochondriacs had significantly higher rates
of lifetime Axis I and Axis II psychiatric disorder than did the
comparison patients; they had more Axis I diagnoses per patient
and more psychopathologic symptoms per patient (elicited in
the DIS interview) (Table 1). Only 11.9% of DSM-III-R hypo¬
chondriacs had no other Axis I lifetime diagnosis, while 48.7%
of the random sample were without any Axis I lifetime disorder
(P<.01). Of the hypochondriacal sample, 35.7% had one other
lifetime DIS diagnosis, 31.0% had two, and 21.4% had three or
more. The comparable figures for the control sample were
27.6%, 15.8%, and 7.9% (all differences between the samples
were significant at the .001 level). The pattern for current
(1-month) psychiatric comorbidity was similar: 21.4% of the hy¬
pochondriacal sample had no other concurrent Axis I diagnosis,
50.0% had one, 16.7% had two, and 11.9% had three or more.
For the comparison sample, 76.3% had no current psychiatric
diagnosis, 18.4% had one, and 5.3% had two diagnoses. These
differences between the samples were all significant at the .001
level. Thus, when primary hypochondriasis was defined as hy¬
pochondriasis without another major concurrent Axis I diagno¬
sis, one fifth of hypochondriacs had the primary condition and
four fifths had the secondary form.
A similar pattern was seen with respect to Axis II disorder. The
*NS indicates not significant.
mean Personality Diagnostic Questionnaire impairment/distress
subscale score of the hypochondriacal sample was three times
that of the comparison sample. Sixty-three percent of the hypo¬
chondriacs had a score of 2 or greater, which has been suggested
as a cutoff for predicting personality disorder caseness.
Tables 2 and 3 present current and lifetime prevalences for
specific disorders. Major depression, dysthymia, generalized
anxiety disorder, and phobias (both current and lifetime) were
common in the hypochondriacal sample and were significantly
more prevalent in them than in the random sample. Panic dis¬
order and antisocial personality, although less prevalent diag¬
noses, were still significantly more common in the hypochon¬
driacal sample. The incidences of alcoholism and substance
abuse, in contrast, did not differ in the two samples. In Table 4,
these data are presented as odds ratios.
Since the taxonomy of the somatoform disorders is particu¬
larly problematic, we examined more closely the relationship
between somatization disorder and hypochondriasis.
prevalence of DSM-III somatization disorder was 21.4% in the
hypochondriacal sample (and 0% in the comparison patients).
The sex ratio of the patients with somatization disorder did
not differ from that of the hypochondriacal sample
whole. If the diagnostic criteria were lowered to 12 symptoms
for women and 10 for men, subthreshold somatization disor¬
der was found in 39.0% of the hypochondriacs. When six and
four symptoms were used, 82.9% of the hypochondriacs meet
these criteria for "abridged" somatization disorder (vs 18.7%
of the comparison sample). The bodily symptoms of somati¬

as assessed with the Whiteley Index
(r=.09). In contrast, hypochondriacal bodily symptoms (as¬
sessed with the Somatic Symptom Inventory)
cantly associated with the Whiteley Index (r=.36; P = .019).

We next contrasted primary and secondary hypochondriasis.
*NS indicates not significant.
Because psychiatric comorbidity was so high, we defined second¬
ary hypochondriasis for this analysis as hypochondriasis accom¬
panied by depressive, anxiety, or somatization disorder. Hypo¬
chondriasis accompanied by other psychiatric diagnoses, those
generally not characterized by prominent somatic features, was
classified as primary hypochondriasis for this analysis. Table 5
shows that primary and secondary hypochondriacs did not differ
in sociodemographic characteristics, functional impairment (the
Functional Status Questionnaire), medical morbidity, or self-
reported sensitivity to bodily sensation (the Somatosensory Am¬
plification Scale). The two groups had similar phénoménologie
characteristics, differing only in greater disease fear among the
A number of cautions must be pointed out before
interpreting these findings. First among these is the rel¬
atively low rate of subject accrual, which raises the pos¬
sibility of selection bias. Sixty-four percent of the patients
above the cutoff on the screening instrument and 54% of
those below it ultimately completed the research battery.
We have no information on those who were not studied
(other than age and sex, which did not differ) and there¬
fore cannot determine if they differed in any systematic
way from those who were studied. Likewise, we do not
have data on those patients who were screened but could
not be contacted or scheduled for the research battery.
Our anecdotal impression was that nonparticipants were
more likely to be employed (and were therefore reluctant
to leave work) and less interested in the financial remu¬
neration. We have no reason to suspect, however, that
this bias operated differently in the hypochondriacal and
Caution must also be employed in generalizing from

order, antisocial personality disorder, and eating disorder were not
calculated because of an insufficient number of cases. NS indicates not
these results because of the relatively small size of the
hypochondriacal sample. Generalizability is also limited

Hypochondriasis and Concurrent
Psychopathologic disorders are prevalent in DSM-III-R
hypochondriacs: their aggregate morbidity was twice as
great as in the random sample of nonhypochondriacal
patients, and a wide array of symptoms and disorders
were significantly more prevalent than in the comparison
group. Only one fifth of the hypochondriacal sample was
without another current Axis I diagnosis. Compared with
other studies,17 we found a relatively low rate of primary
hypochondriasis, ie, of hypochondriasis unaccompanied
by other major disorder. On the other hand, the preva¬
lence of psychiatric disorder in our comparison sample
was within the general range of 25% to 30% for primary
care populations and 40% to 50% for medically ill patients
described in other DIS studies.56'60
The overlap with anxiety and depressive disorders is
most extensive. The odds ratio was 10.1 for generalized
anxiety disorder and 3.3 for phobias. While lifetime panic
disorder was significantly more common among the hy¬
pochondriacs, current panic disorder was not. The true
rate of panic disorder, however, may actually be higher,
because the DIS may be insensitive to panic disorder in
medical patients who tend to somatize their anxiety.61
Hypochondriacs were 8.1 times as likely to have dys-
thymia and 7.1 times as likely to have major depression.
Frequently, the same patient gave a history of both, ie,
"double depression" was common. In contrast, the prev¬
alence of depressive disorder in the random sample fell in
the range of other studies of primary care.56"58
Anxiety Disorder Anxiety Disorder
The literature generally does not suggest an increased
prevalence of alcohol or substance abuse, antisocial per¬
sonality disorder, or eating disorders in hypochondriasis.
These disorders were too uncommon in our samples to
shed light on this question, although lifetime antisocial
Hypochondriacs also had a significantly higher like¬
lihood of Axis II caseness. With two or more positive
responses on the Personality Diagnostic Questionnaire
impairment/distress subscale used to indicate
likelihood of personality disorder, two thirds of the
hypochondriacs may have had a personality disorder.
This is compatible with the clinical impression that
hypochondriasis is an enduring and pervasive pattern
that extends beyond a circumscribed illness which sud¬
denly afflicts a previously normal individual. In addi¬
tion, the hypochondriac's concerns
his or her fears and beliefs seem to them utterly sensi¬
ble and completely justified by their medical predica¬
others who do not believe them and do not take their
somatic experience seriously.62 It can be argued that
the hypochondriac's preoccupation with his
body, health, and symptoms is the patient's most sa¬
lient personality characteristic: a source of self-identity
(hypochondriacs view themselves above all else as peo¬
language, the basis for forming and breaking relation¬
ships and for social intercourse; and a way of reacting
Hypochondriasis and Somatization Disorder
Our findings suggest a considerable but by no means
complete overlap between hypochondriasis and somati¬
zation disorder. Most hypochondriacal patients did not
meet the full DSM-III criteria for somatization disorder.
However, the mean number of somatization disorder
symptoms per patient was sufficiently high, 9.50, that a
small decrease in the diagnostic threshold generated
many more cases. Since some observers think that the
DSM-III criterion of 12 of 14 symptoms is too stringent, the
overlap between the two disorders may be greater than
suggested by our data. At the other extreme, the pro¬
posed "abridged" somatization disorder criterion of six
symptoms for women and four for men64 is so inclusive
that it identifies 25% of the women and 15% of the men
in the comparison group. Thus, although these abridged
criteria may identify an important group of somatizing
patients, they are so numerous that it is unlikely that they
comprise a homogeneous group with a single psychiatric
The data also suggest a possible phénoménologie dis¬
tinction between hypochondriasis and somatization dis¬
order: some functional somatic symptoms may be more
characteristic of somatization disorder and others more
characteristic of hypochondriasis. Although DSM-III so¬
matization disorder symptoms were not significantly cor¬
related with hypochondriacal fears and beliefs, another
group of functional somatic symptoms (the eight Somatic
Symptom Inventory items that are not somatization dis¬
order symptoms) was correlated with hypochondriacal
attitudes. This is consistent with previous work with a
large population of somatizing women in whom two dis¬
tinct symptom patterns were found.29 One resembled
older descriptions of hypochondriasis, and the other was
thought to resemble somatization disorder.
Primary vs Secondary Hypochondriasis
We have found that primary hypochondriasis does ex¬
ist, though it may be relatively uncommon. There are
some DSM-III-R hypochondriacs who have no other co¬
morbid Axis I condition. Few phénoménologie or descrip¬
tive features appear to distinguish hypochondriacs with
anxiety or depressive disorder from those without these
comorbid conditions. The limitations of our data must be
noted here, however, since we did not determine which
comorbid disorder developed first. Our findings are con¬
sistent with those of previous investigations showing few
differences between primary and secondary hypochon¬
driasis. Kenyon1 found no differences in sociodemo-
graphic characteristics, phenomenology, or medical his¬
tory, although primary hypochondriacs
psychiatric histories and less phasic courses. Pilowsky6
found that secondary hypochondriacs had an illness of
shorter duration and had greater fear of disease. We too
found disease fear greater in the secondary hypochondri¬
acs. This may reflect the high prevalence of anxiety dis¬
order in these patients. Disease conviction, on the other
hand, was not significantly higher in secondary hypo¬
chondriasis. This casts some doubt on the suggestion that
depressed hypochondriacs have particularly intense be¬
The high rate of comorbidity seen in this study is con¬
sistent with a growing body of research suggesting that
many psychiatric disorders are not clearly demarcated
and differentiated from each other.65-66 High rates of
comorbidity have recently been reported, for example, in
somatization disorder,67"69 generalized anxiety disorder,
panic disorder,70 and obsessive-compulsive disorder.23
Although DSM-III-R distinguishes among these disorders
and considers them distinct, in clinical reality they tend to
occur together in the same individuals.
Given this general state of affairs, how are we to under¬
stand the patients studied here—patients who feel and be¬
lieve that they are medically sick, but who at the same time
tend to be pathologically anxious or depressed or to have
personality disorders? In these cases of secondary hypo¬
chondriasis, we still need to ascertain which disorder is the
more pervasive or dominant and which first occurs. Being
severely anxious or depressed may lead one to anticipate
the worst and to worry about one's health. Conversely, in¬
tense hypochondriacal concerns and somatic suffering
may be so disruptive and disabling that they lead to ex¬
traordinarily high levels of anxiety and depression. Dis¬
entangling antecedent and consequent, however, can be
very difficult. After extensive research on somatoform
pain disorder and depression, for instance, the nature of
that relationship remains unclear.
Prospective, longitudinal studies wouldobviously help,
but since hypochondriasis is not very prevalent, high-risk
populations would have to be identified. Studies of pa¬
tients with anxiety and depressive disorder could reveal
whether those patients who exhibit hypochondriacal fea¬
tures differ from those who do not. Treatment studies
would help as well: is the successful treatment of panic,
major depression, or phobia accompanied by resolution of
the hypochondriacal symptoms? Finally, further study of
the overlap with personality disorder is of paramount im¬
portance. In many ways hypochondriasis seems more like
a personality characteristic than a circumscribed, discrete
illness. In this sense, hypochondriasis is a way of inter¬
preting one's experience and a way of thinking about
health and illness.62,63 Such a "somatic style" might be
learned in childhood through parental example or paren¬
tal teaching and then might persist largely unameliorated
Primary hypochondriasis might best be understood as
such a hypochondriacal somatic style, while discrete hy¬
pochondriacal episodes occurring in later life may be sec¬
ondary to another psychiatric disorder or to a major life
stress. Primary hypochondriasis would then refer to a
general propensity to worry about health, focus on one's
body, and amplify discomfort. Secondary hypochondria¬
sis would refer to a specific set of functional somatic
symptoms, with their associated fears and beliefs.
The DSM-III represented a major advance in describ¬
ing, defining, and differentiating the somatoform dis¬
orders. Empiric validation of that nosology, using clin¬
ical populations from both medical and psychiatric
settings, is now needed. At present, with
seem prudent not to shift hypo¬
chondriasis to a new position in DSM-IV. In the mean¬
time, this research has important implications for diag¬
nosis and patient care. When treating hypochondriacal
patients, clinicians should suspect additional, although
perhaps less obvious, psychiatric disorders. This is es¬
may themselves be eminently treatable.
